nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reefs, generally found nestled among the tentacles of host anemones (and occasionally large-polyp corals) on shallow reef flats and slopes.

decorPreferences: live rock with crevices for perching
host anemone such as bubble-tip or carpet (optional but appreciated)
stable mature reef with grazing surfaces

diet.notes: Opportunistic feeders that have been documented eating detritus, microalgae, zooplankton, and mucus from host anemones in the wild. In captivity they readily accept small frozen and prepared foods including mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty foods, and crushed flake or pellet. Drop targeted food near their perch as they rarely chase food across the tank.

goodWith: small peaceful reef fish such as gobies and clown gobies
soft corals and LPS in well-fed tanks
avoidWith: lionfish, frogfish, and other predatory fish
any tankmate large enough to swallow a 1–2 cm shrimp

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe, 2368x1776. Tack-sharp, vibrant individual with abdomen raised in the signature display; blue-and-yellow-outlined white saddle spots pop against green host anemone. Best diagnostic and most striking.
Elias Levy, 2137x1425. Sharp near-front view of a whole vibrant specimen on the reef, clearly showing the blue-rimmed white spots and upraised tail; different angle for variety.
Bernard DUPONT, 4416x2649. High-res, sharp; shows the shrimp nestled beside a purple host anemone (its classic association), whole animal with clear diagnostic markings.

summary: The Sexy Shrimp (Thor amboinensis) is a tiny reef shrimp, rarely exceeding 3 cm, native to the Indo-Pacific and Caribbean. Named for its distinctive abdomen-raised tail-wagging display, it is commonly associated with host anemones and is generally regarded as reef-safe when well-fed. Best kept in groups of five or more.
careNotes: Sexy Shrimp are generally considered one of the easiest small ornamental shrimp for a stable, mature reef tank. They are most active and most likely to perform their signature abdomen-wagging display when kept in groups — five or more is commonly advised, and groups in nano reefs often settle near a host anemone, coral, or shaded rock crevice. A host anemone is appreciated but not required; in tanks without one they readily perch on LPS corals, zoanthids, or live rock. Like all marine invertebrates they are highly copper-sensitive and intolerant of elevated nitrate; slow drip-acclimation over an hour or more is generally advised to avoid osmotic shock during introduction. Sufficient iodine levels and stable calcium/alkalinity support successful moulting.
breedingNotes: The Sexy Shrimp has been reported to spawn in captivity, but rearing the planktonic larvae through to settlement is generally considered difficult in standard reef tanks. Females carry visible green eggs under the abdomen and release larvae that drift with the current; without dedicated larval-rearing kreisels (small cylindrical vessels designed to keep fragile larvae in gentle suspension) and live phytoplankton and rotifer feeding, the larvae are typically lost to filtration or consumed by tankmates within days. Captive-bred individuals occasionally appear in the trade.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters and group-size guidance. Salinity stated as 35 ppt (≈ SG 1.026); origin Indonesia; groups of 3 or more recommended.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Third cross-check source. Confirmed family Hippolytidae, adult size ~1 inch, lifespan ~3 years, symbiotic relationships with ~12 anemone species, broad Indo-Pacific/Atlantic distribution, and recommended nano-tank stocking of 3–5 shrimp per 5 gallons.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Adults generally moult roughly monthly; shed exoskeletons should be left in the tank as a calcium source. Sufficient iodine and stable salinity/alkalinity support successful moults. Newly moulted shrimp are vulnerable for a day or two and should have hiding cover.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Females routinely carry eggs in captivity, but the planktonic larvae require dedicated rearing kreisels (cylindrical vessels that hold larvae in gentle suspension) and live microfeeds such as rotifers and phytoplankton to survive. Raising larvae through to settlement is uncommon outside of specialist setups.
